examples.title: Resource Ordering Prevention Algorithm
examples.description: Demonstrates how resource ordering prevents circular wait by enforcing increasing request order
examples.code:
// Resource types with assigned order numbers
// Example: PRINTER=1, TAPE=2, DISK=3

CONSTANT MAX_RESOURCES = 4
VAR resourceOrder[MAX_RESOURCES] = [1, 2, 3, 4]  // PRINTER, TAPE, DISK, CD

FUNCTION requestResource(processID, resourceType, currentHoldings)
BEGIN
    VAR requestedOrder = resourceOrder[resourceType]
    VAR minHoldingOrder = INFINITY
    
    // Find minimum order of currently held resources
    FOR EACH res IN currentHoldings
    BEGIN
        VAR holdOrder = resourceOrder[res]
        IF holdOrder < minHoldingOrder THEN
            minHoldingOrder = holdOrder
        END
    END
    
    // Check if request violates ordering rule
    IF currentHoldings IS EMPTY THEN
        // No holdings, can request any resource
        RETURN ALLOW
    ELSE IF requestedOrder > minHoldingOrder THEN
        // Requesting higher order resource - ALLOW
        RETURN ALLOW
    ELSE
        // Requesting lower or equal order - DENY
        RETURN DENY
    END
END

// Example: Process holds DISK(3) can request CD(4) but not PRINTER(1)
examples.explanation: This algorithm enforces resource ordering to prevent circular wait. A process can only request resources with higher order than what it currently holds. This prevents the formation of circular wait chains that are necessary for deadlock.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of currently held resources

examples.title: Hold and Wait Prevention - Request All Upfront
examples.description: Python implementation showing how requesting all resources upfront prevents Hold and Wait condition
examples.explanation: This implementation prevents Hold and Wait by requiring processes to request all needed resources at once. If all resources are not available, the process waits. This eliminates the 'hold' part of the condition but may lead to low resource utilization.
examples.timeComplexity: O(r) where r is number of resource types

examples.title: No Preemption Prevention with Rollback
examples.description: C program demonstrating resource preemption with checkpointing for deadlock prevention
examples.explanation: This code demonstrates No Preemption prevention. When a process holding resources requests more that cannot be granted, all currently held resources are preempted (returned to the pool). The process state is checkpointed to allow recovery. This prevents deadlock but requires complex checkpointing mechanisms.
examples.timeComplexity: O(h) where h is number of held resources
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for checkpoint storage, where n is process count
